speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number three with Rosette Fischer. What happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother came with us, of course. She came after five years. We became citizen. And my mother, my sister, my brother, they all came to Paterson, New Jersey. My mother, when she sat down outside the house, she said, this is the happiest moment of my life. I feel so good here. She had remarried. And he was a very nice-- we called him Pepe-- very nice gentleman. He loved my children as he loved his own. And we stayed till 1950-- 1962. My husband left. And then in '63, I came with my mother, with my step father, my-- and my little girl. We came to-- we came to Los Angeles. There were more in my house that there were in their own. My gr-- my step father passed away in 19-- oh, about 20 years, 25 years ago, I would say. So my mother was alone. But she was constantly with me. Even now, when I do something in the kitchen-- she used to go around and put an apron around me, say, come on. You're going to get dirty. And I say, oh, come on. Don't bother me. Now, I'm putting on that apron. I just feel her presence all the time. She passed away five years ago. But she will still, she will still be with me as long as I live. I loved my father a lot. I think when I was younger, I loved my father even more than my mother. But what happened to my father happened to many people. And I was young. And it's just-- I got the gift to have my mother that long. And I guess that's why I'm sure that everybody is missing their mother. But I'm missing her so much, because she was that long with me. She was all the life with me, all the time. And that, that's why I miss her so much. And my children loved her a lot, too. They used to call her [? Meme. ?] And even the little girl that doesn't know her, because she was only three months old when my mother passed away, she will talk about her. She thinks she knows her. My mother was a very bright woman. When she heard that somebody got converted, when somebody got adopted, she would say it's the biggest mitzvah that you can do. And I always think about her as a great, great lady. And I wish I could talk to her. And I wish she would have the, the interview instead of me. But I'm speaking in her name. And I want to say that, Ma, we're going to do everything we can for Israel. She loved Israel. She said, without Israel, we can't ex-exist. It will happen the same thing that, that happened before, that happened to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Children, she would say in Yiddish, why are buying another dress, another dress? What do you need so many dresses for? Can't you do with less dresses? Send the money to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was, I remember, a pioneer woman. She was queen many times, because she not only give her money, but she went from house to house to get money. She didn't know how to speak English. But she arrived. She did more than many people that knew how to speak English. She, she belonged. And she made people belong. She made people do things for Israel. And she was always very charitable. When she was younger, she used to work for brides that were-- that didn't, for dowry, for brides. Because she knew how to sew. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rosette, do you still believe in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: It depends. I believe in God. I believe in a, in a higher power. I don't know what, what is that. I, I believe that everybody should believe in the same thing, in being human. And religion shouldn't count that much. But they're forcing us. They're forcing us to-- they're reminding us that we are Jews. And I'm proud to be a Jew. And my, my son went through Yavneh. He graduate Yavneh Academy, not because I'm religious, but because I want to have the tradition. And I want to continue the way my parents lived. And my husband will tell you more about, about God. Because he didn't even want to go into a synagogue. My little boy, I would walk on a holiday in Paterson, New Jersey, with my little boy. And I wanted to go into the synagogue. But he said, go ahead. Go. But I'd not going in. I said, hey, listen. I'm not going to go without you. You have to come with me. I felt very-- I felt strange not to go in. Not that I believe, but I felt that it's my duty. I want to pray for, for, for the one that did not survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask why? Why? I always could never understand why, why things happened the way-- such bad things. There was no reason for it. Nobody hurt anybody. But there is no answer for it. There is no answer why, why I survive, why my sister didn't survive. I have no answer for it. The question is always the same. Why? Why me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never, I never, I never say why I got sick. But I-- because I'm like everybody. It can happen to me. It can happen to anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I'm asking, why are people so bad? Why do they need that power, to have so much power over, over other people? For that matter, they have to kill, they have to do the things, it is-- gas people alive? I, I cannot understand. I still cannot understand. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But I believe in God. Well, I very often say, thank God. I continue the tradition for my mother. I light candles. I'm very proud my son has a Jewish home. He lights candles. My daughter, I get my daughter married not too long ago. She lights candles just in memory of my mother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's like I say, I don't know if it's-- if I believe in God. I think I don't. But there again, there must be something. But why does God have to-- if there is a God, why all this thing happen? God's supposed to be good. So I don't want to say that. Because my, if my grandchildren here listen to me, it would be very bad. But I want them to believe in something. It holds the family together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tradition, I believe, holds the family together. And therefore, I, I want them to be good. And I want them to have a good life. And I worry about them. I worry so much about my grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: You hear so much going on, anti-Semitism all over. And I say to myself, did I have the right? Did I have the right to bring those children to the world? Look, look what's going on. But I hoped that the world will get more intelligent, who knows, more aware of what happened, and that they will see to it that Israel should exist. Because without a country, we had lost.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- nobody can, you know-- you have no identity. Even though America is good, everything is fine, I'm not-- but you're still a Jew. And we have to have Israel. And I'm very-- and if something goes on in Israel, I, I pray. I'm v-- I pray. And I'm proud of people and proud. And I'm grateful to the people that give us Israel. Because they gave their lives. We only give money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my mother, Ester Cyberfarb, and my father, Scmul Cyberfarb. It has been taken sometimes around '20, '20 or '30.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the '30s or in the '20s-- no years, I don't know [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: '40 or [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is taken in France, in the country. The first girl to the left is a friend of mine, [? Jeanette. ?] Then it's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Rosette. The next is my first girlfriend that I met in Paris. Her name was Betty. And the last one to the right is my sister, Rachele. That was in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my little sister, Rachele. She was born in 1926 in Lodz. And she was deported with me to Birkenau, Auschwitz. She died in camp three months later. This is me and a very good friend of mine. His name is [? Fogel, ?] Max [? Fogel. ?] And my name is Rosette. He was arrested in 1942, deported, never came back. I got the picture from his mother. The picture was taken, I believe, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of my family. The, the first to the left is me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rosette?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosette. Next to me is my husband Ralph. Next to my husband is my daughter, Cathy. And above all-- us is my son, Claude. A very happy--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Berkeley when my daughter graduated college, must have been in the '80s. I don't remember. This is my daughter and her husband, Peter. Their name is now Bloch, Peter and Cathy Bloch. They got married two years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out here in Los Angeles-- had a beautiful wedding. And this is my grandson, my joy and pride. His name is Avi. Avi is now 13 years old. He was born in Berkeley-- San Francisco, excuse me, in San Francisco.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose son is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is the son of my son. This is my granddaughter, my sweetheart. And she is seven years old. And her name is named-- she is named after my little sister, Rachelle Lea. What we calling her Lea. She's a little devil. And I love her. This picture is of my mother. She passed away in 1950--

speaker: interviewee
text: 19-- 19-- 1990. She was the love of my life. And I will always remember her. She died in Los Angeles. Blessed-- her memory should be blessed by all of us. Want me to say more? My mother was a very proud lady. And she was s, a big doer, especially for Israel. She loved her children, her grandchildren. And she loved her son-in-laws. That's for sure. I think she loved them more than her daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken, I would say about 20 years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that. I don't recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Los Angeles? It was-- yeah, in Los Angeles. It's just a little picture that we took. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my son and my lovely daughter-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter-in-law's name is Anne, Anne [? Swidler. ?] My son is Claude Fischer. They're both married, happily. They give me a lot of joy. And I'm very proud of my daughter-in-law, and of course of my son. There's no--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taking 19-- what? I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Washington-- their wedding was in Washington.
